instruction: When should you allocate on the stack versus the heap in C++? What are the performance implications and design considerations?
output:
<think>
Stack vs heap allocation is a fundamental C++ decision that affects performance, lifetime, and ownership semantics. Stack allocation is essentially free — it just moves the stack pointer, typically a single instruction. Deallocation is also free — just restoring the stack pointer when the scope exits. Heap allocation (new/malloc) involves the allocator searching for a free block, potentially acquiring locks in multi-threaded programs, and later requiring explicit deallocation or a smart pointer. The performance difference is dramatic — stack allocation is roughly 100-1000x faster than heap allocation for small objects. Cache locality is another factor: stack data is hot in the cache because it's contiguous and recently accessed, while heap allocations scatter data across memory. However, stack has limitations: fixed size (typically 1-8MB), objects must have compile-time known size (no runtime polymorphism without pointers), and lifetime is limited to the enclosing scope. Use the stack for small, short-lived objects with known size. Use the heap for large allocations, objects that outlive their creating scope, or polymorphic objects.
</think>

```cpp
#include <array>
#include <chrono>
#include <iostream>
#include <memory>
#include <vector>

struct Sensor {
    double x, y, z;
    uint64_t timestamp;
};

// STACK: Small, fixed-size, short-lived — always prefer this
void processReading() {
    Sensor reading{1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 12345};  // Stack: ~1 CPU cycle
    std::array<double, 3> coords = {reading.x, reading.y, reading.z};
    // Both destroyed automatically at scope exit — zero overhead
}

// HEAP: Dynamic size, outlives scope, or polymorphic
std::unique_ptr<std::vector<Sensor>> loadSensorLog(size_t count) {
    // count unknown at compile time → heap
    // Vector itself could be on stack, but its internal buffer is heap
    auto log = std::make_unique<std::vector<Sensor>>();
    log->reserve(count);
    for (size_t i = 0; i < count; ++i) {
        log->emplace_back(Sensor{0.0, 0.0, 0.0, i});
    }
    return log;  // Ownership transferred to caller — must outlive scope
}

// BENCHMARK: Stack vs Heap allocation speed
void benchmark() {
    constexpr int ITERATIONS = 1'000'000;

    // Stack allocation
    auto start = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    for (int i = 0; i < ITERATIONS; ++i) {
        Sensor s{1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 0};  // Stack
        volatile double sum = s.x + s.y + s.z;  // Prevent optimization
        (void)sum;
    }
    auto stack_time = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now() - start;

    // Heap allocation
    start = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    for (int i = 0; i < ITERATIONS; ++i) {
        auto s = std::make_unique<Sensor>(Sensor{1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 0});  // Heap
        volatile double sum = s->x + s->y + s->z;
        (void)sum;
    }
    auto heap_time = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now() - start;

    std::cout << "Stack: "
              << std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::microseconds>(stack_time).count()
              << "us\n";
    std::cout << "Heap:  "
              << std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::microseconds>(heap_time).count()
              << "us\n";
    // Typical: Stack ~1ms, Heap ~30-50ms → 30-50x slower
}

// GOTCHA: Stack overflow with large arrays
void dangerousStackUsage() {
    // double huge[1'000'000];  // ❌ ~8MB — may overflow 1MB default stack
    // FIX: Use vector for large dynamic data
    std::vector<double> safe(1'000'000);  // Heap-backed, safe
}

// RULE OF THUMB:
// Stack: sizeof(T) < ~4KB, fixed size, doesn't escape scope
// Heap: large/dynamic size, shared ownership, polymorphism, outlives scope

int main() {
    benchmark();
    processReading();
    auto log = loadSensorLog(1000);
    std::cout << "Loaded " << log->size() << " readings\n";
}
```

instruction: Explain custom allocators in C++ and when they provide meaningful performance benefits. Show a simple pool allocator.
output:
<think>
Custom allocators replace the default new/delete (which typically call malloc/free) with domain-specific allocation strategies. The default allocator is general-purpose — it handles any size, any alignment, any thread, any pattern. This generality has a cost: fragmentation, lock contention, and metadata overhead. Custom allocators exploit domain knowledge: if you know all objects are the same size, a pool allocator eliminates fragmentation and search overhead. If allocations are single-threaded, you skip locks. If all allocations die together, an arena allocator does mass deallocation in O(1). The C++ allocator model integrates with standard containers — you can pass an allocator to vector, map, etc. Since C++17, std::pmr (polymorphic memory resources) makes this much cleaner than the old stateless allocator model. A pool allocator pre-allocates a block of memory and carves it into fixed-size chunks linked in a free list. Allocation is O(1): pop from free list. Deallocation is O(1): push to free list. No fragmentation because all chunks are the same size.
</think>

```cpp
#include <array>
#include <cassert>
#include <cstddef>
#include <iostream>
#include <memory>
#include <vector>

// Simple fixed-size pool allocator
// All allocations are the same size — O(1) alloc and free, zero fragmentation
template <typename T, size_t PoolSize = 1024>
class PoolAllocator {
public:
    using value_type = T;

    PoolAllocator() {
        // Initialize free list: each block points to the next
        for (size_t i = 0; i < PoolSize - 1; ++i) {
            *reinterpret_cast<size_t*>(&pool_[i * sizeof(T)]) = i + 1;
        }
        *reinterpret_cast<size_t*>(&pool_[(PoolSize - 1) * sizeof(T)]) = SIZE_MAX;
        free_head_ = 0;
        allocated_ = 0;
    }

    T* allocate(size_t n) {
        if (n != 1) throw std::bad_alloc();  // Pool only handles single objects
        if (free_head_ == SIZE_MAX) throw std::bad_alloc();  // Pool exhausted

        size_t index = free_head_;
        free_head_ = *reinterpret_cast<size_t*>(&pool_[index * sizeof(T)]);
        allocated_++;
        return reinterpret_cast<T*>(&pool_[index * sizeof(T)]);
    }

    void deallocate(T* ptr, size_t n) {
        if (n != 1) return;
        size_t index = (reinterpret_cast<char*>(ptr) - pool_.data()) / sizeof(T);
        *reinterpret_cast<size_t*>(ptr) = free_head_;
        free_head_ = index;
        allocated_--;
    }

    size_t allocated() const { return allocated_; }
    size_t capacity() const { return PoolSize; }

private:
    std::array<char, PoolSize * sizeof(T)> pool_;
    size_t free_head_;
    size_t allocated_;
};

// Usage example: game entity with pooled allocation
struct Entity {
    float x, y, z;
    int health;
    uint32_t id;

    Entity(uint32_t id, float x, float y)
        : x(x), y(y), z(0), health(100), id(id) {}
};

int main() {
    PoolAllocator<Entity, 256> pool;

    // Allocate entities from pool — O(1) each
    std::vector<Entity*> entities;
    for (uint32_t i = 0; i < 100; ++i) {
        Entity* e = pool.allocate(1);
        new (e) Entity(i, float(i) * 1.5f, float(i) * 2.0f);  // Placement new
        entities.push_back(e);
    }

    std::cout << "Allocated: " << pool.allocated()
              << "/" << pool.capacity() << std::endl;

    // Deallocate — O(1) each, no fragmentation
    for (auto* e : entities) {
        e->~Entity();  // Explicit destructor
        pool.deallocate(e, 1);
    }

    std::cout << "After free: " << pool.allocated() << std::endl;

    // WHEN CUSTOM ALLOCATORS HELP:
    // 1. Game loops: thousands of same-size objects created/destroyed per frame
    // 2. Network servers: per-connection buffer pools
    // 3. Real-time systems: deterministic allocation time (no malloc jitter)
    // 4. Embedded: no heap available, fixed memory budget

    // WHEN THEY DON'T HELP:
    // - Mixed-size allocations (use arena instead)
    // - Infrequent allocations (overhead not worth it)
    // - Already using stack allocation (faster than any allocator)
}
```

instruction: What is arena allocation in C++ and why is it used in compilers, game engines, and network servers? Show a simple arena implementation.
output:
<think>
Arena allocation (also called linear allocation, bump allocation, or region-based allocation) is the simplest and fastest allocation strategy. You have a large pre-allocated block of memory and a pointer that starts at the beginning. To allocate, you just bump the pointer forward by the requested size — one addition, no searching, no metadata. Deallocation of individual objects doesn't happen — instead, you free the entire arena at once when you're done with a phase of work. This is perfect for phase-based workloads: a compiler allocating AST nodes for one function (free all when done), a game engine allocating per-frame scratch data (reset at frame end), a web server allocating per-request data (free when response sent). The key insight is that many workloads have natural lifetime phases where everything allocated in one phase dies together. Arena allocation makes this O(1) regardless of how many objects were allocated. The trade-off is you can't free individual objects — it's all or nothing. Modern C++ can implement this with std::pmr::monotonic_buffer_resource.
</think>

```cpp
#include <cassert>
#include <cstddef>
#include <cstdlib>
#include <cstring>
#include <iostream>
#include <new>

class Arena {
public:
    explicit Arena(size_t capacity)
        : buffer_(static_cast<char*>(std::malloc(capacity)))
        , capacity_(capacity)
        , offset_(0)
        , allocation_count_(0) {
        if (!buffer_) throw std::bad_alloc();
    }

    ~Arena() { std::free(buffer_); }

    // Allocation: just bump the pointer — O(1)
    void* allocate(size_t size, size_t alignment = alignof(std::max_align_t)) {
        // Align the current offset
        size_t aligned = (offset_ + alignment - 1) & ~(alignment - 1);
        if (aligned + size > capacity_) {
            throw std::bad_alloc();  // Arena exhausted
        }
        void* ptr = buffer_ + aligned;
        offset_ = aligned + size;
        allocation_count_++;
        return ptr;
    }

    // Typed allocation with construction
    template <typename T, typename... Args>
    T* create(Args&&... args) {
        void* mem = allocate(sizeof(T), alignof(T));
        return new (mem) T(std::forward<Args>(args)...);
    }

    // Free everything at once — O(1) regardless of allocation count
    void reset() {
        offset_ = 0;
        allocation_count_ = 0;
        // Note: destructors NOT called — arena types must be trivially destructible
        // or you must track and call destructors manually
    }

    size_t used() const { return offset_; }
    size_t remaining() const { return capacity_ - offset_; }
    size_t allocations() const { return allocation_count_; }

    // No copy/move
    Arena(const Arena&) = delete;
    Arena& operator=(const Arena&) = delete;

private:
    char* buffer_;
    size_t capacity_;
    size_t offset_;
    size_t allocation_count_;
};

// Example: Compiler AST nodes allocated in an arena
struct ASTNode {
    enum Kind { NUM_LIT, BIN_OP, VAR_REF };
    Kind kind;
    union {
        double number;
        struct { ASTNode* left; ASTNode* right; char op; } binop;
        const char* name;
    };
};

ASTNode* parseExpression(Arena& arena) {
    // All AST nodes for this function live in the arena
    auto* left = arena.create<ASTNode>();
    left->kind = ASTNode::NUM_LIT;
    left->number = 42.0;

    auto* right = arena.create<ASTNode>();
    right->kind = ASTNode::VAR_REF;
    right->name = "x";  // String must also be arena-allocated in real code

    auto* root = arena.create<ASTNode>();
    root->kind = ASTNode::BIN_OP;
    root->binop = {left, right, '+'};

    return root;
}

int main() {
    Arena arena(1024 * 1024);  // 1MB arena

    // Simulate compiling multiple functions
    for (int i = 0; i < 1000; ++i) {
        auto* ast = parseExpression(arena);
        // ... compile AST to bytecode ...
        (void)ast;
        arena.reset();  // Free ALL nodes at once — O(1)!
    }

    std::cout << "Arena capacity: " << arena.remaining() << " bytes free\n";

    // USE CASES:
    // Compilers: AST per-function, freed after codegen
    // Games: per-frame scratch (particles, UI, physics temp data)
    // Web servers: per-request allocations, freed on response
    // Parsers: token/node allocation, freed after parse tree consumed
}
```

instruction: Explain memory alignment in C++ and placement new. When do alignment issues cause real problems, and how does placement new give you control over object construction?
output:
<think>
Memory alignment is a hardware requirement — CPUs access memory most efficiently when data is at addresses that are multiples of the data's size. A 4-byte int should be at an address divisible by 4, an 8-byte double at an address divisible by 8. Misaligned access on some architectures (ARM, older x86) causes a hardware fault; on modern x86 it works but with a performance penalty (splitting across cache lines). C++ handles alignment automatically for normal variables, but when you manage raw memory (custom allocators, memory-mapped I/O, SIMD), you need to handle it yourself. alignas specifies alignment requirements, alignof queries them. Placement new constructs an object at a specific memory address without allocating — you provide the memory, placement new calls the constructor. This is essential for custom allocators, memory pools, and shared memory. The combination of proper alignment and placement new gives you full control over where and how objects are constructed, which is required for high-performance systems.
</think>

```cpp
#include <cstddef>
#include <cstdlib>
#include <cstring>
#include <iostream>
#include <new>       // Required for placement new
#include <memory>

// === ALIGNMENT BASICS ===

struct Unpadded {
    char a;     // 1 byte
    // 7 bytes padding (to align next member)
    double b;   // 8 bytes, requires 8-byte alignment
    char c;     // 1 byte
    // 7 bytes padding (to make struct size multiple of max alignment)
};
// sizeof(Unpadded) = 24, not 10!

struct Packed {
    double b;   // 8 bytes
    char a;     // 1 byte
    char c;     // 1 byte
    // 6 bytes padding
};
// sizeof(Packed) = 16 — save 8 bytes by reordering!

// SIMD requires 16 or 32-byte alignment
struct alignas(32) SimdVector {
    float data[8];  // 256-bit AVX register
};
static_assert(alignof(SimdVector) == 32);

// === PLACEMENT NEW: Construct at a specific address ===

struct Connection {
    int fd;
    std::string address;
    bool active;

    Connection(int fd, std::string addr)
        : fd(fd), address(std::move(addr)), active(true) {
        std::cout << "Connection constructed: " << address << "\n";
    }

    ~Connection() {
        std::cout << "Connection destroyed: " << address << "\n";
    }
};

void placementNewDemo() {
    // Allocate raw memory — no constructor called
    alignas(Connection) char buffer[sizeof(Connection)];

    // Construct in-place using placement new
    Connection* conn = new (buffer) Connection(42, "192.168.1.1");

    std::cout << "FD: " << conn->fd << ", Addr: " << conn->address << "\n";

    // CRITICAL: Must call destructor manually — no delete!
    conn->~Connection();
    // Do NOT call delete conn — memory wasn't from new
}

// === Practical: Object pool using aligned storage + placement new ===

template <typename T, size_t N>
class AlignedPool {
public:
    T* construct_at(size_t index, auto&&... args) {
        if (index >= N) throw std::out_of_range("pool index");
        if (alive_[index]) throw std::logic_error("slot occupied");

        T* ptr = reinterpret_cast<T*>(&storage_[index]);
        new (ptr) T(std::forward<decltype(args)>(args)...);  // Placement new
        alive_[index] = true;
        return ptr;
    }

    void destroy_at(size_t index) {
        if (index >= N || !alive_[index]) return;
        reinterpret_cast<T*>(&storage_[index])->~T();  // Manual destructor
        alive_[index] = false;
    }

    ~AlignedPool() {
        for (size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
            destroy_at(i);
        }
    }

private:
    // alignas ensures proper alignment for T
    alignas(T) std::byte storage_[N][sizeof(T)];
    bool alive_[N] = {};
};

int main() {
    std::cout << "sizeof(Unpadded): " << sizeof(Unpadded) << "\n";  // 24
    std::cout << "sizeof(Packed): " << sizeof(Packed) << "\n";      // 16
    std::cout << "alignof(SimdVector): " << alignof(SimdVector) << "\n"; // 32

    placementNewDemo();

    AlignedPool<Connection, 4> pool;
    auto* c1 = pool.construct_at(0, 10, "10.0.0.1");
    auto* c2 = pool.construct_at(1, 11, "10.0.0.2");
    pool.destroy_at(0);  // Destructor called, slot freed
    auto* c3 = pool.construct_at(0, 12, "10.0.0.3");  // Reuse slot
}
```
